contributions.statement: A Tool Manager component that enables LLMs to learn how to use existing bioinformatics tools and web APIs from documentation without requiring new code implementations.
contributions.quote: The Tool Manager agent addresses this by enabling LLMs to learn how to use existing tools from documentation covering descriptions, inputs, outputs and dependencies rather than rewriting new codes.

contributions.statement: BioMed-AQA — a benchmark comprising 327 biomedical data analysis tasks across five categories (omics analysis, precision medicine support analysis, machine learning, statistical analysis, and data visualization) for evaluating LLM agents.
contributions.quote: To comprehensively evaluate the performance of BioMedAgent, we developed BioMed-AQA, a benchmark that includes 327 biomedical data analyses questions across five categories: omics analysis (O), precision medicine support analysis (P), machine learning (M), statistical analysis (S) and data visualization (V)

contributions.statement: Systematic case-study evaluation of eight open-source AI frameworks on scientific research reproduction tasks reveals none can complete a full research cycle from literature understanding through computational execution to validated results and scientific writing.
contributions.quote: We tested eight open-source AI frameworks (Agent Laboratory, AutoGen, BabyAGI, GPT Researcher, MOOSE-Chem2, SciAgents, SciMON, and Virtual Lab) on two tasks that aimed to reproduce research on algorithm development from recent papers in uncertainty quantification and protein interaction discovery. In our evaluation, no framework completed a full research cycle from literature understanding through computational execution to validated results and scientific paper writing.

contributions.statement: AI research frameworks demonstrate a critical capability gap: competence in conceptual planning and summarization but consistent failure at robust computational implementation, with all frameworks producing sophisticated hallucinations.
contributions.quote: While all systems showed competence in conceptual tasks such as planning and summarization, they consistently failed at robust implementation. Every framework produced sophisticated hallucinations.

contributions.statement: Definition of a full research cycle spanning literature understanding, hypothesis formulation, computational implementation, experiment execution with controls, result validation, theoretical interpretation, and scientific writing—operationalized as a holistic evaluation framework for assessing autonomous AI research capabilities beyond isolated benchmarks.
contributions.quote: we adopt a working notion of a full research cycle as encompassing a series of essential capabilities: understanding existing scientific literature with sufficient depth to identify knowledge gaps, formulating testable hypotheses and experimental plans, implementing computational methods through correct code, executing experiments with proper controls and parameter settings, obtaining and validating numerical results, interpreting findings within theoretical frameworks and identifying limitations, and producing clear scientific writing that accurately represents methods and results.

contributions.statement: AI research frameworks require substantial debugging and technical expertise to deploy in practice, contradicting claims about democratization of science, though they show potential value as supervised assistants for methodological planning and ideation.
contributions.quote: Deployment proved demanding, requiring substantial debugging and technical expertise, which undermines common claims about the democratization of science with AI. Despite these limitations, the frameworks showed promise as research assistants for methodological planning and ideation under careful human supervision.

contributions.statement: An open-source framework enabling autonomous agent laboratories to collaboratively develop research by uploading and retrieving findings from a shared preprint server, allowing agents to iteratively build upon each other's discoveries.
contributions.quote: We introduce AgentRxiv —a novel, open-source framework designed to archive and disseminate research outputs from autonomous agents. This platform enables agents to build upon the discoveries of other agents, driving iterative improvements over time.

contributions.statement: Autonomous agents with access to their prior research achieve measurable cumulative performance improvements across research iterations, with accuracy on mathematical reasoning benchmarks improving from 70.2% to 78.2% through newly discovered reasoning techniques.
contributions.quote: We show that each generation of papers produces measurable improvements when agents are given access to AgentRxiv. For instance, accuracy on the MATH-500 benchmark steadily increased from a 70.2% baseline to 78.2% (11.4% relative improvement) using newly discovered techniques like Simultaneous Divergence Averaging (SDA).

contributions.statement: Reasoning strategies discovered by agents on a source benchmark generalize across diverse target benchmarks and multiple language models, with consistent performance improvements averaging 3.3%.
contributions.quote: We demonstrate that reasoning strategies discovered through AgentRxiv on MATH-500 generalize to other benchmarks and language models. Using the highest performing discovered reasoning method by AgentRxiv, our experiments show performance increase across a range of tasks—from GPQA and MMLU-Pro to MedQA—while also revealing consistent gains across five different language models, from DeepSeek-v3 to Gemini-2.0 pro (improving on average by 3.3%).

contributions.statement: Parallelized execution mode for autonomous agent laboratories, allowing multiple agents to run simultaneously while sharing research findings through a centralized platform, accelerates cumulative research progress compared to isolated agent development.
contributions.quote: We introduce a parallelized mode for AgentRxiv, allowing multiple agentic systems to run simultaneously and share findings. We show that this setup accelerates improvements on MATH-500 by +6.0% with 3 parallel labs. We also find that there are a trade-offs between speed and computational efficiency, with discoveries occurring faster but at a higher computational cost.

edges.evidence: Both describe autonomous AI scientist systems producing discoveries; B's scaling result on discovery cycles is consistent with and supports the feasibility claim of A, though they are different systems.
Both claim autonomous AI scientists can generate novel validated discoveries; B provides supporting evidence for the broader autonomous discovery thesis, though different systems.
B's claim that AI-generated manuscripts pass peer review at a top-tier ML workshop supports A's claim of autonomous scientific discovery in ML reaching meaningful quality.
B is explicitly The AI Scientist-v2, a successor system that eliminates human-authored templates and adds agentic tree-search, directly extending the original AI Scientist in A.
Both A and B describe the same AI Scientist system with near-identical descriptions of the fully automated end-to-end pipeline.
AI-Researcher is a different autonomous multi-agent research pipeline pursuing the same goal; parallel work supporting the same general capability claim.
B releases artifacts from autonomous ML research attempts, plausibly the AI Scientist's outputs, supporting reproducibility of the system described in A.
Both describe full-process autonomous AI scientist systems producing scientific discoveries; B's finding supports the general feasibility claim of A.
Both claim fully autonomous AI output meets a meaningful conference/workshop quality bar; B's human-evaluated peer-review passing supports A's automated-reviewer acceptance claim.
B's automated reviewer matching human accuracy provides the evaluation tool whose verdict underpins A's claim that AI papers exceed the acceptance threshold.
Both describe multi-agent automated scientific discovery frameworks with overlapping pipeline stages; B's human-AI framework is a parallel approach corroborating feasibility.
Both are end-to-end research agents covering idea-to-paper pipeline; MLR-Agent is a comparable baseline system in the same space.
Both are end-to-end autonomous discovery multi-agent systems; parallel systems corroborating the approach.
B is a public release of artifacts from autonomous ML research attempts, plausibly using/analyzing AI-Scientist-type systems, related to A's system.
Both are fully autonomous multi-agent systems covering the complete research pipeline; closely parallel contributions.
B's claim that a full-process multi-agent system produces meaningful discoveries (with a gap to humans) corroborates A's autonomous discovery system feasibility.
B reframes fully autonomous science (as in A) into a tractable mentor-guided baseline-extension task, narrowing the problem setting.
Both claim AI-generated manuscripts can pass first-round peer review at a workshop of a top ML conference; near-duplicate corroborating claims.
Both analyze AI-generated manuscripts passing peer review at ML conference workshops; A's analysis corroborates B's claim.
Both concern AI-generated feedback/writing quality relative to humans in scientific writing; complementary observations.
Both benchmark current capabilities/limitations of autonomous AI scientific writing through expert/peer-review-like evaluation; B provides corroborating evidence on the same question.
A is the code/data release for AI Scientist-v2, which is the successor extending The AI Scientist (B).
Both provide evidence that autonomous AI research output can reach meaningful quality bars (peer-review scores / passing peer review), broadly agreeing on the feasibility claim.
Both report autonomous multi-agent/AI scientist systems producing meaningful scientific output, mutually reinforcing feasibility while noting quality gaps.
Both report autonomous AI scientists achieving validated quality scientific output, broadly consistent evidence for AI-driven discovery feasibility.
A explicitly compares against and improves on prior fully automated systems restricted to single-file code (The AI Scientist), extending that pipeline approach.
A's risk taxonomy cataloguing failure modes is supported/complemented by B's defenses improving safety; both address AI-scientist safety, B addresses risks of the kind A catalogues.
Both concern safety risks in AI scientific systems; A taxonomizes risks, B benchmarks high-risk scientific tasks, broadly aligned and complementary.
Both derive from end-to-end autonomous ML research experience; B's design principles complement A's risk taxonomy, addressing the same failure-mode space constructively.
Both catalog failure modes in AI scientist systems; B's methodological failure modes overlap with and reinforce A's taxonomy.
Both describe LLM-driven iterative research ideation by extracting problems and synthesizing approaches from literature; B's approach aligns with A's methodology.

Both build concept co-occurrence knowledge structures from scientific literature for idea/research generation; B's concrete co-occurrence knowledge graph instantiates the entity-centric store idea in A.
Both claim LLM reviewers with structured criteria/rubrics align with human judgement and serve as scalable proxies; mutually supporting findings.
Both concern human-preference-aligned LLM review evaluation as scalable proxy; B's preference-trained predictor supports A's claim of human-aligned LLM feedback.
B's finding that researchers value LLM feedback as competitive with human review supports A's claim that LLM reviewing agents provide human-aligned, useful feedback.
A claims LLM reviewing feedback is human-preference-aligned and improves quality; B finds LLM-as-judge scores diverge from expert ratings with near-zero correlation, challenging that alignment.
A relies on LLM peer review producing useful feedback; B's finding that researchers find LLM feedback useful supports the underlying premise.
B shows LLM review overlaps with human reviews at human-human rates, supporting A's use of LLM-based peer review validation.
B finds augmenting data-driven generation with retrieved literature performs best, supporting A's claim that literature and data hypotheses are complementary.
B proposes integration methods combining knowledge and data sources, extending A's premise that these are complementary.
Both are datasets/benchmarks for novelty assessment; B's expert-annotated dataset relates to A's benchmark, plausibly similar resources.
A finds LLMs have limited novelty understanding/gaps with humans; B shows expert in-context examples improve LLM novelty evaluation, consistent with A's finding that LLMs need help.
Both report LLM novelty judgments are unreliable/misaligned with human or empirical assessment, mutually supporting.
A benchmarks LLMs on novelty evaluation; B provides an expert-annotated novelty dataset to support such evaluation. A's benchmarking work plausibly builds on such datasets.
A identifies prompting strategies improving novelty evaluation; B shows expert in-context examples improve novelty evaluation accuracy — consistent empirical findings.
A's analysis and fine-tuning strategies for novelty evaluation could build on B's expert-annotated novelty dataset.
PaperQA2 is explicitly the successor agent extending PaperQA's RAG tool-based design.
LitQA2 is explicitly the successor benchmark extending LitQA's full-text retrieval evaluation.
PaperQA2 is evaluated on full-literature retrieval tasks like LitQA; the benchmark supports evaluation of such agents. Same lineage (LitQA used to evaluate PaperQA).
Both describe agent-based RAG systems for scientific literature that match or exceed expert/strong-LLM performance; B (PaperQA2) is likely the same or closely related system as A.
A describes a RAG agent matching expert humans in accuracy/time at lower cost; B (PaperQA2) is the agent achieving expert-level literature performance. Strongly aligned, likely same work.
B provides the human-AI comparison methodology with unrestricted human baselines used to establish A's claim of matching experts in accuracy and time.
B (LitQA2) is a full-literature retrieval benchmark with direct human comparison, matching A's claim of matching experts on a full-text retrieval benchmark.

A is a perturbation framework for diagnosing LLM peer-review robustness; B evaluates many LLMs on peer review revealing capability gaps. Both assess LLM peer-review capabilities, plausibly complementary.
Both inject perturbations/errors into peer review components to diagnose LLM reviewer weaknesses; A is a more structured framework of the general idea B describes.

Both relate to scaling agent compute/iteration for cumulative scientific reasoning improvements.

Both involve multi-agent scaling improving scientific outcomes; A's results support that multi-agent approaches improve performance.

B claims LLMs can summarize technical documents and inform experimental design; A's CFD paper-parameter extraction is a concrete instance of such document summarization capability.
Both describe modular/in-context mechanisms to augment agent capabilities with domain expertise without retraining; complementary approaches to dynamic capability augmentation.

Both describe extensible/modular mechanisms for augmenting agent tools/capabilities without architectural re-engineering or workflow-specific redesign — convergent design goals.

Both are LLMs fine-tuned to generate structured academic reviews; convergent goals in same task space.
Both concern LLM-based peer review systems and their evaluation; convergent domain and goals.

Both are benchmarks for evaluating scientific review/feedback generation; complementary same-purpose datasets.
A explicitly states it merges DeepReview-Bench (B) with AgentReview to build ScholarEval, so A extends B.
Both are benchmarks evaluating AI research/ML agents on end-to-end tasks; parallel benchmark efforts in adjacent domains.
Both are benchmarks for evaluating LLM/AI agents on research/ML tasks across multiple domains; parallel efforts.
Both find that general-purpose agents perform competitively/strongly on biomedical ML tasks (with degradation on hard cases), consistent findings.

A states LLM agents underperform on biomedical ML workflows; B presents a framework aimed at autonomous biomedical analyses. B is a proposed solution motivated by the problem in A, but the link is loose.

Both are benchmarks of ~100 problems for AI agents in genomics/computational biology with multi-step workflows; GeneBench appears to extend similar evaluation concept, but they may be parallel independent benchmarks. Possibly supports.
Both concern synthesizing/verifying QA data to produce unambiguous, verifiable answers for benchmarks; methodologically aligned.

A reports agentic systems' accuracy on compbio benchmarks with degradation on hard problems; this is plausibly a finding from evaluating on GeneBench-like benchmark B. Empirical finding supports the benchmark's utility.
Both are empirical findings about agent performance on biomedical/compbio tasks; A about drivers of success, B about degradation on hard problems. Loosely complementary.
Both use ontologies/knowledge graphs to ground and guide LLM hypothesis generation, reducing ungrounded reasoning; methodologically aligned approaches.
Both employ ontologies and graph-based representations to structure scientific hypotheses/reasoning; complementary methods.
Both embed knowledge graphs/ontologies into LLM agent frameworks for scientific automation; related approaches to grounding agents.
Both claim multi-agent AI systems can produce scientific discoveries, though they make somewhat different claims about the quality gap vs humans. Loosely supportive.
Both describe multi-agent scientific discovery frameworks; B's full framework with curation and review could be seen as elaborating the multi-agent discovery approach in A. Related but distinct.
B is a multi-agent discovery framework; A is a material discovered via such automated multi-agent discovery, serving as a concrete instance/evidence.
A claims multi-agent AI can produce discoveries; B is an example of a discovery from automated multi-agent science. Loosely supportive.
Both assert LLMs are capable of scientific research tasks (hypothesis generation, cross-concept inference); mutually supporting.
A is optimistic about LLM hypothesis generation; B emphasizes critical obstacles limiting LLMs in replicating research. Partial tension but not strictly incompatible.
A provides experimental validation of LLM-generated hypotheses outperforming baselines, supporting B's claim that LLMs generate testable hypotheses.
A is a benchmark for LLM hypothesis generation that would quantify the capability B claims; supportive/evaluative relation.
Both reformulate peer review as iterative/multi-round process to capture real-world iterative nature; strongly aligned claims.
Both are structured frameworks for deeper peer review; loosely aligned on improving review depth though different mechanisms.
Both are peer review benchmarks/datasets derived from venues like ICLR for evaluating review generation; loosely supportive of the same evaluation goal.
ReviewMT supports multi-turn dialogue review systems, which aligns with A's iterative multi-round review approach; loosely supportive.

Both concern peer-review evaluation; B's claim about multimodal review aligns with A's suite that assesses MLLM peer-review capabilities, though A is a benchmark and B a finding.
Both describe agent architectures enabling sustained long-horizon autonomous research; share the theme of deep iteration and scaling action depth.
Both describe scalable agent architectures for sustained scientific reasoning workflows; complementary approaches to scaling agent execution.
Both concern scaling autonomous agent research execution; A via hierarchical depth, B via parallelization. Complementary themes.

Both describe agentic systems autonomously generating hypotheses and conducting scientific discovery; complementary instances of automated discovery in different domains.
Both describe AI/multi-agent systems for autonomous scientific discovery with human validation in the loop, broadly aligned approaches.
Both identify failure modes/obstacles limiting LLM agents in scientific research tasks, including unreliable code/results.
Both report limitations of current agents in scientific coding/replication tasks, mutually reinforcing.
Both are automated metrics/frameworks for assessing idea novelty/distinctness relative to prior work; complementary.
A operationalizes novelty via embeddings; B offers facet-based novelty definition. A's novelty scoring could use B's facet framing, loosely related.
Both concern LLM novelty evaluation; A's benchmarking finding aligns with B's operationalization of novelty assessment.
Both relate to benchmarking/operationalizing academic novelty assessment; complementary.
Both concern LLM novelty evaluation methodology; A's prompting analysis aligns with B's facet operationalization.
Both demonstrate hybrid hypothesis generation (LLM plus structured/causal knowledge) outperforming LLM-only, mutually reinforcing.
Both describe combining knowledge-driven and data-driven sources for hypothesis generation; B is an instance of A's integration spectrum.
Both integrate structured/ontological knowledge into LLM hypothesis generation to reduce hallucination and improve grounding; they agree on the same approach and goal.

B reports specific novel interactions discovered via automated hypothesis validation; A describes the automated validation method matching PhD performance. B appears to be results from the same validation approach.
Both propose frameworks/benchmarks for evaluating LLM scientific idea generation, with structured dimensions and rigorous comparison; they address the same evaluation problem.

Both use retrieval grounded in literature; A's aspect-based retrieval pipeline and B's RAG limitation identification share methodology, but for different tasks (novelty validation vs limitation finding). Weak overlap.
Both are retrieval-augmented LLM frameworks grounding analysis in prior literature; A targets novelty, B targets limitations. Methodologically related but different tasks.
Entity-centric knowledge store using concept co-occurrences for idea generation aligns with entity-linking-based refinement improving hypothesis quality; both leverage entity relations.
Both describe multi-agent LLM systems for autonomous scientific work; A's evolving multi-agent system achieving novelty aligns with B's claim that mixture-of-agents enables disruptive discovery.
A provides empirical evidence that multi-agent collaboration improves idea innovation/impact, which supports B's claim that mixture-of-agents architecture facilitates disruptive discovery.
A shows AI manuscripts can pass peer review, which loosely supports B's claim that human-AI collaborative QC can assure validity; weak.
A shows LLM judges diverge from expert ratings, qualifying/adding a condition to B's claim that LLM-based assessment combined with experts assures validity.
A's reviewing agents aligned with human judgements improving ideas supports B's notion of human-AI collaborative quality control.

A's H-Max metric calibrating AI critiques against human reviews supports B's human-AI hybrid quality assurance concept.
A's framework includes human curation and adversarial review, consistent with B's human-AI collaborative quality control.
A finds LLM novelty judgments poorly correlated with effectiveness, undermining LLM-as-judge; B reports LLM ideas scoring high in novelty/feasibility taking such scores at face value — tension.

A claims AI feedback can be superior to human reviews; B is a benchmark for evaluating review/feedback generation. Loosely related as B could evaluate the kind of system in A, but distinct contributions.
A reports AI hypotheses producing validated outperforming algorithms; B reports AI failing to propagate diagnostic signals into decisions. Tension but on different aspects.

A evaluates LLM review capabilities; B reports researchers find LLM feedback competitive with human review. Both inform LLM review utility.

Both concern e-processes/e-values for valid inference; B's inferential shell with e-processes and validity certification could implement/extend A's e-value sequential testing framework. Genuine topical overlap.
MLEvolve uses retrospective memory and self-evolution; B is a memory retrieval algorithm for reuse and self-evolution. B could be a component of/related to A's memory mechanism.

A claims a fully autonomous system completes the entire research pipeline; B finds no open-source framework can complete a full research cycle. Genuine tension.
Both evaluate autonomous AI research agents on reproduction/implementation tasks; B's findings about framework limitations align with A's benchmark purpose.

A claims an autonomous AI scientist successfully reproduces findings and generates novel validated discoveries; B claims no open-source AI framework can complete a full research cycle. These present conflicting assessments of autonomous AI research capability.

Both come from empirical case-study experience; A's design principles respond to the same practical deployment challenges B documents.
